title: Pseudogout (CPPD)
oneLiner: Acute calcium pyrophosphate crystal arthritis in elderly, mimicking septic arthritis; must aspirate joint to exclude infection before treating.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Septic arthritis** — the single finding that says "this is not routine pseudogout": **synovial fluid Gram stain shows organisms OR culture is positive**. If febrile, toxic, or prosthetic joint, treat as septic until proven otherwise.
- **Acute crystalline-induced systemic inflammation** — in frail elderly, severe attack can cause high fever, rigors, and hypotension mimicking sepsis. Look for **crystals on polarized light microscopy** but start antibiotics if any doubt (cultures will guide cessation).
sections.Presentation:
- Acute monoarticular hot, swollen, painful joint (knee >> wrist, ankle, elbow)
- Erythema and warmth over joint (often mistaken for cellulitis)
- Reduced range of movement due to pain
- May have low-grade fever (rarely high fever)
- Can be triggered by recent surgery, trauma, illness, or new diuretic
- **Key exam finding:** tender effusion with marked restriction of passive motion (same as septic arthritis – cannot clinically differentiate)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Septic arthritis** – Gram stain + culture; more likely if fever >38.5°C, rigors, single joint, rapid progression, immunocompromise
- **Gout (monosodium urate)** – negatively birefringent needle-shaped crystals; often first metatarsophalangeal joint, but can look identical
- **Osteoarthritis flare** – no effusion or minimal, less erythema, chronic background pain, X-ray shows joint space loss and osteophytes
- **Rheumatoid arthritis flare** – usually polyarticular + symmetrical, morning stiffness, positive anti-CCP, inflammatory markers high
- **Reactive arthritis** – recent gastroenteritis or genitourinary infection, often lower limb oligoarthritis, conjunctivitis, urethritis
- **Traumatic haemarthrosis** – history of trauma, blood in joint, no crystals

**Splitter:** Gram stain and culture of synovial fluid. Positive = septic arthritis. Negative + crystals = crystal arthropathy. Do not rely on fever or inflammatory markers alone.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):** **Joint aspiration** – send fluid for urgent Gram stain, culture, polarised light microscopy, and crystal analysis. Use a sterile technique; if septic suspicion is high, start empiric antibiotics immediately after aspirate (do not delay for result).
- **Bloods:** FBC, CRP, U+E, LFT, bone profile (Ca, Mg, PO4), urate, ferritin, TFT, iron studies (to identify underlying metabolic triggers). Check blood cultures if febrile.
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** X-ray of affected joint (weight-bearing if knee) – look for chondrocalcinosis (linear calcification in hyaline cartilage or fibrocartilage, e.g., meniscal calcification in knee, triangular fibrocartilage in wrist, symphysis pubis). Absence does not rule out pseudogout.
  - **Second-line:** Ultrasound or MRI if joint not accessible for aspiration (e.g., hip) or if suspect osteomyelitis/abscess. Ultrasound may show crystal deposits, effusion, synovitis.
- **Special tests:** Polarised light microscopy – rhomboid, positively birefringent calcium pyrophosphate crystals.
- **Diagnostic criteria:** No validated scoring system. Diagnosis rests on **synovial fluid demonstration of CPP crystals** plus typical acute arthritis. Chronic CPPD arthritis diagnosed by X-ray findings and clinical picture.
- **Never-miss test:** **Synovial fluid Gram stain and culture** – to rule out septic arthritis. This investigation must be done **before any steroid injection or systemic treatment**.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour/ED):**
  - If septic arthritis not excluded, start empir intravenous antibiotics (e.g., flucloxacillin 2 g IV QDS if penicillin allergy – [check local antimicrobial policy]) after aspiration. Review when culture results return.
  - Analgesia: paracetamol 1 g QDS PO/IV. Consider weak opioid (codeine 30–60 mg QDS) if severe.
  - For confirmed pseudogout (crystals + negative Gram stain/culture), manage pain and inflammation.
- **Ward (first 24–72 h):**
  - First-line anti-inflammatory: NSAIDs (e.g., naproxen 500 mg BD PO or ibuprofen 400 mg TDS PO) – but **avoid if eGFR <30, active PUD, or elderly with multiple risk factors**. Always prescribe PPI cover (omeprazole 20 mg OD) when using NSAIDs.
  - Alternative: colchicine 500 mcg PO BD–TDS for acute attack (max 6 mg total course); stop if diarrhoea or vomiting. Adjust dose in renal impairment (eGFR <30 – avoid or halve dose; [check local policy]).
  - If NSAIDs and colchicine contraindicated: intra-articular steroid injection (e.g., methylprednisolone acetate 40 mg for large joint, 20 mg for medium joint – after confirming no infection). For polyarticular or refractory cases: oral prednisolone 30–40 mg OD for 3–5 days, then taper by 10 mg every 2–3 days.
  - Treat underlying cause: check Ca, Mg, PO4, ferritin. If hypercalcaemia → investigate for primary hyperparathyroidism. If low Mg → replace (MgSO4 IV or Mg glycerophosphate PO). If haemochromatosis → refer to gastroenterology.
- **Long-term/outpatient:**
  - No specific disease-modifying drug for CPPD.
  - Advise avoidance of known triggers (major surgery, dehydration, trauma, thiazide/loop diuretics).
  - If >3 attacks/year: consider colchicine prophylaxis 500 mcg PO OD (but limited evidence; monitor renal function).
  - Manage underlying osteoarthritis with physiotherapy, weight loss, joint protection.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: 1–2 L/day oral or IV 0.9% saline if NBM or dehydrated. No specific type.
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD if not anticoagulated or high bleeding risk.
  - Diet: as tolerated (NBM only if awaiting aspiration or surgery).
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-hourly (more frequent if febrile or septic concern).
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line:**
  - Naproxen 500 mg PO BD (with PPI) for 3–5 days
  - Or ibuprofen 400 mg PO TDS (with PPI) for 3–5 days
- **Colchicine:**
  - Acute attack: 500 mcg PO 2–4 times daily until pain relief or GI upset, max total 6 mg per course. **(Check local policy for elderly/renal – many protocols advise lower doses)**
- **Corticosteroids:**
  - Intra-articular: methylprednisolone acetate 40 mg (knee) to 20 mg (wrist/ankle)
  - Oral: prednisolone 30–40 mg OD for 3–5 days, taper over 1–2 weeks
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - NSAIDs – avoid in severe CKD, active GI bleeding, concomitant anticoagulants (warfarin/DOACs)
  - Colchicine – do not use with macrolide antibiotics (e.g., clarithromycin) or azole antifungals (inhibits CYP3A4, increases toxicity); avoid in severe hepatic impairment
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Colchicine – eGFR 30–59: reduce dose (use 500 mcg OD max); eGFR <30: avoid or halve dose; [check local policy]
  - NSAIDs – avoid if eGFR <30; use with caution if eGFR 30–59, monitor U+E, stop if rise in creatinine
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Prescribing colchicine in moderate–severe renal impairment (eGFR <30) without dose reduction** – leads to myelosuppression, multiorgan failure, and death. Also: **giving NSAIDs to elderly patient with CKD and peptic ulcer without PPI** – acute kidney injury + GI bleed.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Pseudoseptic presentation (high fever, rigors) – can delay appropriate management
  - Joint destruction if recurrent severe attacks (rare)
- **Chronic:**
  - Chronic CPPD arthritis (inflammatory OA, often symmetrical wrists/ knees)
  - Joint deformity, functional limitation
  - Association with OA – accelerated joint degeneration
sections.Prognosis: - Acute attacks typically resolve in 3–7 days with treatment; recurrence is common. Chronic CPPD leads to progressive joint damage, but mortality is low (worse if underlying metabolic disease goes untreated).
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- Always **aspirate a hot swollen joint** before treating – do not rely on X-ray chondrocalcinosis alone.
- **Check calcium, magnesium, phosphate, ferritin** once – pseudogout can be a sentinel event for hyperparathyroidism, haemochromatosis, or hypomagnesemia.
- In elderly patients with acute pseudogout, consider **urine microscopy and blood cultures** (occult UTI can trigger attack).
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Assuming X-ray chondrocalcinosis = pseudogout** – many elderly have incidental chondrocalcinosis without symptoms.
- **Treating with NSAIDs or steroids before ruling out septic arthritis** – co-infection occurs (crystals + positive culture). Always send culture.
- **Using colchicine in acute attack beyond recommended course** – toxicity risk without benefit.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** worsening pain, fever >38°C, inability to weight-bear, spreading erythema, rigors, or new joint involvement within 24–48 hours.
- **Follow-up:** primary care or rheumatology clinic in 2–4 weeks if frequent attacks or underlying cause found. If on colchicine prophylaxis, recheck U+E after 1 month.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior/ICU if:** NEWS2 ≥5, lactate >2 mmol/L, GCS <15, hypotension (SBP <90), anuria, or suspicion of septic shock with joint source.
- **Threshold for orthopaedic referral:** prosthetic joint, suspected septic arthritis, inability to aspirate joint, or recurrent large effusions requiring drainage.
- **If toxic despite negative synovial fluid Gram stain** – consider meta-analysis (both crystals and infection can coexist) – start antibiotics and escalate to rheumatology/infectious disease.

rawMarkdown:
## Pseudogout (CPPD)

**One-liner:** Acute calcium pyrophosphate crystal arthritis in elderly, mimicking septic arthritis; must aspirate joint to exclude infection before treating.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Septic arthritis** — the single finding that says "this is not routine pseudogout": **synovial fluid Gram stain shows organisms OR culture is positive**. If febrile, toxic, or prosthetic joint, treat as septic until proven otherwise.
- **Acute crystalline-induced systemic inflammation** — in frail elderly, severe attack can cause high fever, rigors, and hypotension mimicking sepsis. Look for **crystals on polarized light microscopy** but start antibiotics if any doubt (cultures will guide cessation).

### Presentation
- Acute monoarticular hot, swollen, painful joint (knee >> wrist, ankle, elbow)
- Erythema and warmth over joint (often mistaken for cellulitis)
- Reduced range of movement due to pain
- May have low-grade fever (rarely high fever)
- Can be triggered by recent surgery, trauma, illness, or new diuretic
- **Key exam finding:** tender effusion with marked restriction of passive motion (same as septic arthritis – cannot clinically differentiate)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Septic arthritis** – Gram stain + culture; more likely if fever >38.5°C, rigors, single joint, rapid progression, immunocompromise
- **Gout (monosodium urate)** – negatively birefringent needle-shaped crystals; often first metatarsophalangeal joint, but can look identical
- **Osteoarthritis flare** – no effusion or minimal, less erythema, chronic background pain, X-ray shows joint space loss and osteophytes
- **Rheumatoid arthritis flare** – usually polyarticular + symmetrical, morning stiffness, positive anti-CCP, inflammatory markers high
- **Reactive arthritis** – recent gastroenteritis or genitourinary infection, often lower limb oligoarthritis, conjunctivitis, urethritis
- **Traumatic haemarthrosis** – history of trauma, blood in joint, no crystals

**Splitter:** Gram stain and culture of synovial fluid. Positive = septic arthritis. Negative + crystals = crystal arthropathy. Do not rely on fever or inflammatory markers alone.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):** **Joint aspiration** – send fluid for urgent Gram stain, culture, polarised light microscopy, and crystal analysis. Use a sterile technique; if septic suspicion is high, start empiric antibiotics immediately after aspirate (do not delay for result).
- **Bloods:** FBC, CRP, U+E, LFT, bone profile (Ca, Mg, PO4), urate, ferritin, TFT, iron studies (to identify underlying metabolic triggers). Check blood cultures if febrile.
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** X-ray of affected joint (weight-bearing if knee) – look for chondrocalcinosis (linear calcification in hyaline cartilage or fibrocartilage, e.g., meniscal calcification in knee, triangular fibrocartilage in wrist, symphysis pubis). Absence does not rule out pseudogout.
  - **Second-line:** Ultrasound or MRI if joint not accessible for aspiration (e.g., hip) or if suspect osteomyelitis/abscess. Ultrasound may show crystal deposits, effusion, synovitis.
- **Special tests:** Polarised light microscopy – rhomboid, positively birefringent calcium pyrophosphate crystals.
- **Diagnostic criteria:** No validated scoring system. Diagnosis rests on **synovial fluid demonstration of CPP crystals** plus typical acute arthritis. Chronic CPPD arthritis diagnosed by X-ray findings and clinical picture.
- **Never-miss test:** **Synovial fluid Gram stain and culture** – to rule out septic arthritis. This investigation must be done **before any steroid injection or systemic treatment**.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour/ED):**
  - If septic arthritis not excluded, start empir intravenous antibiotics (e.g., flucloxacillin 2 g IV QDS if penicillin allergy – [check local antimicrobial policy]) after aspiration. Review when culture results return.
  - Analgesia: paracetamol 1 g QDS PO/IV. Consider weak opioid (codeine 30–60 mg QDS) if severe.
  - For confirmed pseudogout (crystals + negative Gram stain/culture), manage pain and inflammation.
- **Ward (first 24–72 h):**
  - First-line anti-inflammatory: NSAIDs (e.g., naproxen 500 mg BD PO or ibuprofen 400 mg TDS PO) – but **avoid if eGFR <30, active PUD, or elderly with multiple risk factors**. Always prescribe PPI cover (omeprazole 20 mg OD) when using NSAIDs.
  - Alternative: colchicine 500 mcg PO BD–TDS for acute attack (max 6 mg total course); stop if diarrhoea or vomiting. Adjust dose in renal impairment (eGFR <30 – avoid or halve dose; [check local policy]).
  - If NSAIDs and colchicine contraindicated: intra-articular steroid injection (e.g., methylprednisolone acetate 40 mg for large joint, 20 mg for medium joint – after confirming no infection). For polyarticular or refractory cases: oral prednisolone 30–40 mg OD for 3–5 days, then taper by 10 mg every 2–3 days.
  - Treat underlying cause: check Ca, Mg, PO4, ferritin. If hypercalcaemia → investigate for primary hyperparathyroidism. If low Mg → replace (MgSO4 IV or Mg glycerophosphate PO). If haemochromatosis → refer to gastroenterology.
- **Long-term/outpatient:**
  - No specific disease-modifying drug for CPPD.
  - Advise avoidance of known triggers (major surgery, dehydration, trauma, thiazide/loop diuretics).
  - If >3 attacks/year: consider colchicine prophylaxis 500 mcg PO OD (but limited evidence; monitor renal function).
  - Manage underlying osteoarthritis with physiotherapy, weight loss, joint protection.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: 1–2 L/day oral or IV 0.9% saline if NBM or dehydrated. No specific type.
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD if not anticoagulated or high bleeding risk.
  - Diet: as tolerated (NBM only if awaiting aspiration or surgery).
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-hourly (more frequent if febrile or septic concern).

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line:**
  - Naproxen 500 mg PO BD (with PPI) for 3–5 days
  - Or ibuprofen 400 mg PO TDS (with PPI) for 3–5 days
- **Colchicine:**
  - Acute attack: 500 mcg PO 2–4 times daily until pain relief or GI upset, max total 6 mg per course. **(Check local policy for elderly/renal – many protocols advise lower doses)**
- **Corticosteroids:**
  - Intra-articular: methylprednisolone acetate 40 mg (knee) to 20 mg (wrist/ankle)
  - Oral: prednisolone 30–40 mg OD for 3–5 days, taper over 1–2 weeks
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - NSAIDs – avoid in severe CKD, active GI bleeding, concomitant anticoagulants (warfarin/DOACs)
  - Colchicine – do not use with macrolide antibiotics (e.g., clarithromycin) or azole antifungals (inhibits CYP3A4, increases toxicity); avoid in severe hepatic impairment
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Colchicine – eGFR 30–59: reduce dose (use 500 mcg OD max); eGFR <30: avoid or halve dose; [check local policy]
  - NSAIDs – avoid if eGFR <30; use with caution if eGFR 30–59, monitor U+E, stop if rise in creatinine
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Prescribing colchicine in moderate–severe renal impairment (eGFR <30) without dose reduction** – leads to myelosuppression, multiorgan failure, and death. Also: **giving NSAIDs to elderly patient with CKD and peptic ulcer without PPI** – acute kidney injury + GI bleed.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Pseudoseptic presentation (high fever, rigors) – can delay appropriate management
  - Joint destruction if recurrent severe attacks (rare)
- **Chronic:**
  - Chronic CPPD arthritis (inflammatory OA, often symmetrical wrists/ knees)
  - Joint deformity, functional limitation
  - Association with OA – accelerated joint degeneration

### Prognosis
- Acute attacks typically resolve in 3–7 days with treatment; recurrence is common. Chronic CPPD leads to progressive joint damage, but mortality is low (worse if underlying metabolic disease goes untreated).

### Pearls (positive actions)
- Always **aspirate a hot swollen joint** before treating – do not rely on X-ray chondrocalcinosis alone.
- **Check calcium, magnesium, phosphate, ferritin** once – pseudogout can be a sentinel event for hyperparathyroidism, haemochromatosis, or hypomagnesemia.
- In elderly patients with acute pseudogout, consider **urine microscopy and blood cultures** (occult UTI can trigger attack).

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Assuming X-ray chondrocalcinosis = pseudogout** – many elderly have incidental chondrocalcinosis without symptoms.
- **Treating with NSAIDs or steroids before ruling out septic arthritis** – co-infection occurs (crystals + positive culture). Always send culture.
- **Using colchicine in acute attack beyond recommended course** – toxicity risk without benefit.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** worsening pain, fever >38°C, inability to weight-bear, spreading erythema, rigors, or new joint involvement within 24–48 hours.
- **Follow-up:** primary care or rheumatology clinic in 2–4 weeks if frequent attacks or underlying cause found. If on colchicine prophylaxis, recheck U+E after 1 month.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior/ICU if:** NEWS2 ≥5, lactate >2 mmol/L, GCS <15, hypotension (SBP <90), anuria, or suspicion of septic shock with joint source.
- **Threshold for orthopaedic referral:** prosthetic joint, suspected septic arthritis, inability to aspirate joint, or recurrent large effusions requiring drainage.
- **If toxic despite negative synovial fluid Gram stain** – consider meta-analysis (both crystals and infection can coexist) – start antibiotics and escalate to rheumatology/infectious disease.
